coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles based immunoassay for detection of alpha-L-fucosidase
coredata.dc:creator: Mintz, Keenan
Waidely, Eric
Zhou, Yiqun
Peng, Zhili
Al-Youbi, Abdulrahman O.
Bashammakh, Abdulaziz S.
El-Shahawi, Mohammad S.
Leblanc, Roger M.
coredata.dc:description: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is among the leading causes of mortality in the world. The detection of HCC in its early stage is the key for early treatment and thus the improvement of the chances of survival. Among the various methods of HCC screening, assays based on the detection of biomarker that is specific to HCC such as alpha-l-fucosidase (AFU) have been regarded as the most prominent methods. In this regards, a new assay for the detection of AFU to screen HCC was developed. This assay was based on the energy transfer between carbon dots (C-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), the concentration of AFU could be monitored by the degree of C-dots fluorescence quenching due to the energy transfer. With this assay, a limit of detection of 3.4 nM (well below the diagnostic cutoff point of 80 nM), and a broad linear range of detection from 11.3 to 200 nM were achieved. We also demonstrate the determination of the concentration of AFU in human blood serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hepatocellular carcinoma
Alpha-l-fucosidase
Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antibody functionalization with fluorescein isothiocyanate probe
AuNPs-based assay for detection of AFU in PBS buffer
AuNPs assay for detection of AFU in blood serum
AuNPs immunoassay optimization with bovine IgG
AuNPs immunoassay for detection of AFU in PBS buffer
AuNPs immunoassay for detection of AFU in human blood serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Simplified, schematic illustration for the detection of AFU.

UV-Vis absorption spectra of citrate capped AuNPs (black) and IgG capped AuNPs (red), the optical path length of the cell is 10 mm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of polyclonal anti-BSA capped AuNPs with FITC labeled monoclonal anti-BSA (ex. 494 nm), incubated with increasing concentrations of BSA and (B) Stern-Vølmer linear extrapolation. The optical path length of the cell is 10 mm.
Normalized emission spectra of C-dots at various excitation wavelengths.

Overlap between absorbance of polyclonal-capped AuNPs (black), emission of FITC probe conjugated to monoclonal anti-BSA (red), and emission of C-dots labeled with monoclonal anti-BSA (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of polyclonal anti-BSA capped AuNPs with C-dots labeled monoclonal detection antibody, incubated with increasing concentrations of BSA (ex. 460 nm) and (B) Stern-Vølmer linear extrapolation.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of polyclonal anti-FUCA2 capped AuNPs with C-dots labeled monoclonal detection antibody, incubated with increasing concentrations of AFU (ex. 460 nm) and (B) Stern-Vølmer linear extrapolation. All the samples were incubated in PBS buffer of pH = 7.4.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of polyclonal anti-FUCA2 capped AuNPs with C-dots labeled monoclonal detection antibody, incubated with increasing concentrations of AFU (ex. 460 nm) and (B) Stern-Vølmer linear extrapolation. All the samples were incubated in human blood serum.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles based immunoassay for detection of alpha-L-fucosidase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Miami, 1301 Memorial Drive, Coral Gables, FL, 33146, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, King Abdulaziz University, P.O. Box 80200, Jeddah, 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is among the leading causes of mortality in the world. The detection of HCC in its early stage is the key for early treatment and thus the improvement of the chances of survival. Among the various methods of HCC screening, assays based on the detection of biomarker that is specific to HCC such as alpha-l-fucosidase (AFU) have been regarded as the most prominent methods. In this regards, a new assay for the detection of AFU to screen HCC was developed. This assay was based on the energy transfer between carbon dots (C-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), the concentration of AFU could be monitored by the degree of C-dots fluorescence quenching due to the energy transfer. With this assay, a limit of detection of 3.4 nM (well below the diagnostic cutoff point of 80 nM), and a broad linear range of detection from 11.3 to 200 nM were achieved. We also demonstrate the determination of the concentration of AFU in human blood serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is among the leading causes of cancer mortality in the world, it is estimated that over 90% of the liver cancers are HCC [1–3]. Current methods for the detection of HCC include computerized tomography (CT) scan, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and ultrasonography. However, these methods often fail to discover tumor formation in the early stages due to the limitation in their detectable tumor size (typically > 1 cm) [4]. It is of great importance and interest to develop methodologies that are capable detecting HCC in an early stage, which could lead to early treatment and thus improve the chances of survival. Among the various approaches, detections based on biomarker that is specific to HCC such as alpha-l-fucosidase (AFU) [5,6], have been regarded as the most prominent methods. AFU is a lysosomal enzyme and its levels in healthy adults are found to be 22.8 ± 7.1 U/L (∼50 nM) [7]; however it is generally overexpressed in cancerous tissues and a concentration higher than 35 U/L (80 nM) in patients' blood would indicate the occurrence of HCC [8]. It's been reported that about 85% of HCC patients were diagnosed up to 6 months before it is detectable by ultrasonography.

Many approaches have been utilized in developing an efficient biosensor for the detection of AFU, and the development of an effective assay relies upon a balance among the sensitivity of detection, ease of approach, the speed in which detection can be made, and the cost. Radioimmunoassay is among the most sensitive techniques, however, they require highly trained individuals and the use of hazardous isotopes [9]. In the past two decades, various methods have been developed to detect AFU exploiting the enzymatic activity of AFU. In these assays, fucose-containing substrates (i.e., 2-chloro-4-nitrophenyl- α-l-fucopyranoside) are undergo hydrolytic cleavage reactions in the presence of AFU; destruction of the substrate [10], or formation of new products thus generate various type of signal changes such as electric potential [11], and fluorescence intensity [12–14], which could be used to qualitatively or quantitatively detect AFU. However, these assays generally require a nonphysiological, acidic medium (i.e., pH = 5.0) which is not convenient for blood sample screening; also, the sensitivity and selectivity of these assays often suffer from interference substances (i.e., ions, small molecules and proteins), which is inevitable due to the fact these assays are based on enzymatic activity of AFU. Assays with high sensitivity towards AFU have been reported, however they suffer from very narrow linear range of detection, which could not differentiate the difference of AFU levels in healthy and HCC patients [14,15]. Thus, an assay that is able to function under physiological conditions (i.e., 37 °C and pH 7.4), with high sensitivity, selectivity and broad range of detection is still desired for AFU detection and early HCC screening.

Recently, the development of nanotechnology has shown great promise in furthering the field of analytics [16]. Among the various types of nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have recently attracted significant interest due to their superior properties [17,18]. Compared to other similar nanoparticles, they tend to have better water dispersibility [19,20], low toxicity [21], high biocompatibility [22,23], and ease in synthetic approaches [24]. The most attractive quality of C-dots, however, is their optical properties. C-dots are generally highly luminescent with stable photoactivity and experience excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions. This allows the tuning of emission of C-dots to a desired range by simply selecting a corresponding excitation. Further, their optical properties could also be fine-tuned by modify their surface chemistry or change the size [25]. Tunable emissions are a very desirable trait in sensing, as the emission can be catered to best suit the detection method. Thus, C-dots have been considered as one of the ideal materials for sensor development [26]. Meanwhile, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are some of the most investigated classical nanoparticles, and they possess very favorable properties, especially for optics [27]. AuNPs express a large absorption band around 525 nm, caused by the surface plasmon [28].

As our continuous efforts in developing novel analytical methods [29–31], we previously reported an assay by incorporating immunoglobulin (IgG) onto quartz plate through bifunctional linkers for the detection of AFU [29]; however this assay requires extensive modification on the quartz plate. To develop an assay with less modifications and easier operations, we explored the possibility using C-dots to develop an assay for the detection of AFU. We envisioned that by coupling AuNPs and C-dots in the same assay, the emission of C-dots could be tuned, such that, there will be a spectral overlap in the emissions of C-dots with the surface plasmon band of the AuNPs. Once this is achieved, an assay can be developed in which AuNPs will come into close proximity with C-dots in the presence of the biomarker, leading to a quenching of the C-dots luminescence through energy transfer to the surface plasmon band of the AuNPs. This approach could be then used to produce a calibration curve that can be utilized in quantifying unknown concentrations of the respective biomarker such as AFU. As the assay is based on the highly specific interaction of AFU and its corresponding antibody (IgG anti-FUCA2), we expect it will work under physiological conditions with high selectivity, sensitivity and broad detection range. Here we report our findings using antibody-assisted, AuNPs and C-dots based assay, which provides a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.4 nM for AFU under physiological conditions. The linear range of this method is between 11.3–200.0 nM, sufficiently cover AFU levels of both healthy and HCC patients. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time where an assay based on C-dots and AuNPs has been developed for the detection of AFU.

Chloroauric acid and bovine IgG were obtained from MP Biomedicals (Santa Ana, CA, USA). Citric acid (anhydrous, ≥99.5%), sodium citrate (95%), ethylenediamine (99%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and human blood serum were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO) and were used without further purification. PD 10 columns were received from GE Healthcare Bio-Sciences (Pittsburg, PA, USA). Dialysis was run through a Cellulose Ester membrane with molecular weight cut-off 500–1000 Da purchased from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (Rancho Dominguez, CA). Purified water was obtained from an Elga PURELAB Ultra water purifier (Woodridge, IL) with a resistivity of 18 MΩ·cm, surface tension of 72.6 mN m−1, and pH 5.6 at 20.0 ± 0.5 °C.

Centrifugation was taken on an Allegra 64R from Beckman Coulter (Brea, CA, USA). Ultraviolet-Visible spectroscopy measurements were obtained on an Agilent Cary 100 spectrophotometer (Santa Clara, CA, USA). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 (Kyoto, Japan) with slit widths set to 5 nm for both excitation and emission.

The synthesis of AuNPs was carried out according to a reported procedure with slight modifications [32], briefly: 50 mL of a 0.01% (w/v) solution of chloroauric acid dissolved in deionized (DI) H2O was brought to a boil. Once boiling, 2 mL of 1% sodium citrate (w/v) was added under constant, vigorous stirring. The mixture was left boiling for 10 min, then moved to cool in the dark. The final product collected was diluted up to 50 mL and stored in the dark at 4 °C until further use.

Sodium carbonate was added to the citrate-capped AuNPs to adjust the pH to 9. 1 mL of the citrate-capped AuNPs (pH = 9) was then incubated with 1 mL of 0.15 mg/mL polyclonal anti-BSA IgG or anti-FUCA2 in PBS buffer for 90 min at room temperature. The product was then immediately cooled down to 4 °C. Once cooled, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 30 min at same temperature (4 °C). The pellet of IgG-capped AuNPs obtained after centrifugation is then re-suspended in PBS and stored in the dark at 4 °C.

To prepare the C-dots probe, citric acid and ethylenediamine were condensed into polymeric C-dots possessing excitation-dependent emission according to our previously reported method after slight modification [33]. The synthesis began by heating 20 mL of ethylenediamine to a boil (>120 °C). Once the ethylenediamine began to boil, approximately 4 g of citric acid was added to give a molar ratio of 14:1 ethylenediamine to citric acid. The mixture was then refluxed for ∼10 min. After this time period, the residual ethylenediamine was then evaporated off using a rotovap. Upon evaporation, a viscous yellow crude product was left behind. To remove excess starting material or other impurities, the crude product was dissolved in a minimal amount of DI water and then put under dialysis (molecular weight cut-off 500–1000) with 2 L of water for 2 days, the water was changed every 4–10 h. The C-dots were found to be spherical particles with an average size of 3.50 nm; all of the characterizations (see Fig. 4 and Fig. S2) are in agreement with our previously published results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibody functionalization with fluorescein isothiocyanate probe
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL of a 2 mg/mL solution of the desired antibody (anti-BSA or FUCA2) was incubated with 1.5 equivalents of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) dissolved in DMSO. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, then separated using a PD-10 column (Sephadex G-25) with Tris buffer (pH 8.2) to elute the conjugated product. The final concentration is determined by absorption spectroscopy.

A 0.01 mg/mL solution of monoclonal anti-BSA or anti-FUCA2 is dissolved in PBS (pH 7.4). A separate solution is then prepared, with 1 g of C-dots dissolved in 2 mL of MES buffer with 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC). Next, the EDC activated C-dots are moved to a vial containing 1.5 equivalents of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) dissolved in PBS buffer. The vial is sealed and argon is bubbled in and the vial is left to stir at room temperature for 2 h in the dark. After 2 h, the 0.01 mg/mL solution of monoclonal antibody is added. The contents are left stirring at room temperature for 1 h, then left to incubate at room temperature for two days. To separate the labeled antibody, a PD 10 desalting column with Sephadex G25 was used. The column is equilibrated with Tris buffer (pH = 7.4), and then the antibody/C-dots solution is added to the column. Tris buffer is added to elute the C-dots labeled antibodies, and the conjugation is confirmed through spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs-based assay for detection of AFU in PBS buffer
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of the IgG-capped AuNPs were incubated with 100 μL of 0.005 M NaCl in 500 μL of PBS, containing known amounts of AFU. The nanoparticles were incubated with AFU at 37 °C for 30 min. 200 μL of the monoclonal C-dots labeled detection antibody was then added and the incubation was continued another 30 min. The mixture was then placed under centrifugation for 10 min at 10,000 RPM. The supernatant was removed and the pellet was re-suspended in fresh PBS. The solution was then placed under centrifugation two additional times for 10 min at 10,000 RPM, removing the supernatant and suspending the pellet in fresh PBS each time. The fluorescence of the C-dots probe was measured where the intensity without AFU was denoted as F0 and that in the presence of AFU as F, then a plot of the fluorescence intensity ratios F0/F vs. the concentration of AFU was obtained.

To optimize the incubation time for the method, three assays using identical procedures as discussed above in 2.7 were developed, except their incubation time varied, which were 15, 30 and 60 min, respectively. These three assays were then compared based on their r2 values in the calibration curves (see supporting information Fig. S3) to determine the best incubation time to use. When the incubation time was decreased to 15 (15 + 15) minutes, the linearity was affected as seen in the decrease of the r2 value to 0.956. When the incubation time was lengthened to 60 (60 + 60) minutes the linearity increased as indicated by an R2 value of 0.978. This is comparable to the r2 value for the incubation with 30 (30 + 30) minutes. Since there is no significant increase in linearity for the increase of the incubation time from 30 to 60 min, the incubation time of 30 (30 + 30) minutes was chosen for further assays, because 30 min incubation was sufficient for the interaction of the antigen (AFU) and antibody (anti-FUCA2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs assay for detection of AFU in blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of the IgG-capped AuNPs were incubated with 500 μL of 0.005 M NaCl in 500 μL of blood serum, containing known amounts of AFU. The nanoparticles were incubated with AFU at 37 °C for 30 min. 200 μL of the monoclonal C-dots labeled detection antibody was then added and the incubation was continued another 30 min. After incubation, a 10 min period was taken to allow the mixtures to cool to room temperature. Then the fluorescence of the C-dots probe was measured and a plot of F0/F vs. the concentration of AFU was obtained.

Briefly, AuNPs immunoassay was constructed by employing polyclonal antibody-capped AuNPs for efficient capture of the desired antigen (i.e., AFU); while another immunoassay was constructed by conjugating C-dots to a monoclonal detection antibody to improve the specificity [34]. Without any AFU, AuNPs and C-dots are generally not interacting with each other and there should be no optical changes. However, in the presence of AFU, upon antigen (AFU) binding to the polyclonal antibody-capped AuNPs, a sandwich-type assay can be produced by addition of the monoclonal antibody-functionalized C-dots. Since the emission wavelength of C-dots can be easily tuned to overlap with the surface plasmon of the AuNPs, AFU (the antigen) binding will cause a decrease in photoluminescence of C-dots due to energy transfer to the surface plasmon, thus realizing the detection of AFU (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs immunoassay optimization with bovine IgG
ce:section.ce:para: To develop an immunoassay based on AuNPs support, detection of bovine serum albumin (BSA) using a fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) probe was first attempted for proof of concept. Preparation of AuNPs began with the boiling of tetrachloroauric acid with sodium citrate to yield citrate-capped AuNPs, which averaged 22.7 nm in size according to our TEM and AFM measurements (see Fig. S1 in supplementary materials). The citrate-capped AuNPs were then incubated in a solution containing polyclonal anti-BSA at a high pH. Alkaline pH ensures that the primary amines on the polyclonal antibody are not fully protonated, allowing for electrostatic interactions to occur with the AuNPs surface. Over time, the citrate capping is replaced with antibody capping, which was confirmed through observing the absorption spectrum of the two prepared nanoparticles. AuNPs with citrate capping display only one peak that appears at 525 nm from the surface plasmon of the AuNPs (Fig. 2 , black line). After treatment with the polyclonal IgG, an additional peak is observed at 280 nm, which indicates the presence of the antibody. Additionally, the surface plasmon was found to experience a red shift and weaken in signal upon antibody binding (Fig. 2, red line).

In preparing the antibody-capped AuNPs, a polyclonal antibody was chosen as the capture antibody. Polyclonal antibodies are cheaper to produce than monoclonal antibodies and possess affinity to multiple epitopes on antigens [34]. While affinity to multiple epitopes can lead to a decrease in specificity, it can greatly enhance the amount of antigen that is captured in solution. Detection of antigen binding can then be enhanced for sensitivity by using a probe labeled with monoclonal antibody for the same antigen. The monoclonal antibodies are highly specific and express affinity to a single epitope, allowing the desired antigen to be discriminated from nonspecific binding that may occur with other antigens on the capture antibody [34].

To produce a detection antibody, monoclonal anti-BSA was labeled with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC). FITC was chosen as a probe due to its favorable binding chemistry to antibodies and its emission at ∼520 nm which is very close to the plasmon peak of AuNPs (∼525 nm). The emission of the chosen probe is important for the purpose of detection in the assay. The assay is focused upon the strong absorbance of the surface plasmon of the AuNPs. When the detection antibody binds to the antigen, it is brought into closer proximity of the AuNPs and the overlap with the surface plasmon results in a decrease in the fluorescence emission of the probe due to the energy transfer.

To determine if the energy transfer from FITC-labeled detection antibody to AuNPs could be sufficient in quantifying the concentration of antigen in solution, the polyclonal anti-BSA-capped AuNPs were incubated in increasing concentrations of BSA. After incubation with BSA for 30 min at 37 °C, the monoclonal FITC-labeled anti-BSA was added and the incubation was continued for an additional 30 min at 37 °C. Fig. 3 A shows the emission spectra of FITC in varying concentrations of BSA, as well as a control in which no BSA was added (CPS means counts per second, it's the number of photons detected by fluorometer detector, which could be used to evaluate fluorescence intensity). The value obtained with no BSA displayed the highest emission intensity as expected, and this value was used as the initial fluorescence (F0) to extrapolate a Stern-Vølmer plot. Fig. 3B displays the linear extrapolation at varying concentrations of BSA. The y-intercept of the plot goes approximately through 1, which is expected, as with no antigen present F0/F would be equal to 1. However, the degree of linearity expressed from the r2 value is only 0.88, indicating the data points have a degree of improvement necessary, which might be attributed to the inefficient overlap between the FITC emission and the plasmon absorption of AuNPs.

According to the study in Fig. 3, it was clear that improvements needed to be made to the assay. One way found to enhance detection was to utilize an alternative probe. FITC is a standard probe in labeling biomolecules but new classes of probes have since been established. C-dots are a relatively new class of probes that have been found to have value in biosensing. One advantage that C-dots possess over FITC is that the emission of C-dots is generally dependent upon the excitation wavelength. This allows the emission of the C-dots to be tuned to a desired range by selecting the appropriate excitation wavelength (Fig. 4 ), making the emission of C-dots to experience high overlap with the absorbance band of the AuNPs. With a greater amount of overlap, the absorbance of the emission from the probe will be more efficient and in turn, the assay will be more efficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs immunoassay for detection of AFU in PBS buffer
ce:section.ce:para: Upon confirming the methodology with BSA, the AuNPs assay was modified for detection of AFU. The only necessary modification for detecting AFU was to change the polyclonal capping antibody and the monoclonal detection antibody to IgG with specificity towards the FUCA2 epitope on AFU. The AuNPs immunoassay for detecting AFU was run by incubating polyclonal anti-FUCA2-capped AuNPs in increasing amounts of AFU in PBS at 37 °C for 30 min. After 30 min of incubation, the C-dots labeled monoclonal anti-FUCA2 detection antibody was added to the incubation. The incubation was carried out for an additional 30 min, then the mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was removed. The AuNPs pellet was re-suspended in PBS and the emission spectrum of the C-dots probe was measured for each concentration of AFU. Fig. 7 shows plot of F0/F vs. the concentration of AFU. An ideal Stern-Vølmer plot would cross the y-intercept at 1 and be perfectly linear. The plot for AFU detection deviates slightly, as the y-intercept was found to be 0.999. The linearity displayed was 0.975, indicating there is indeed a strong correlation with the intensity of fluorescence of the C-dots probe and the concentration of AFU present. According to well-accepted 3σ method [35,36], the LOD of this assay was determined to be 3.4 nM. When we further increased the concentration of AFU (from 100 nM till 1000 nM), we observed the saturation of signals when AFU concentration is larger than 200 nM (Fig. S4 A and B, supporting information), however, the assay has very good linearity when AFU concentration is less than 200 nM. The limit of quantitation (LOQ) is the threshold at which the calibration curve can be used to confidently determine the concentration of analyte and was determined to be 11.3 nM [36,37], thus the linear range of this assay is from 11.3 nM to 200 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs immunoassay for detection of AFU in human blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: After confirming the applicability of the current methodology for the detection of AFU in PBS buffer, we moved to our final target: the detection of AFU in human blood. Due to the presence of rich substances such as cholesterol, glucose, proteins (i.e., hemoglobin), etc. in human blood, an assay that works with PBS buffer may not necessarily succeed in human blood. Thus it is very important to demonstrate the above assay in human blood considering its potential practical applications. Fig. 8 shows the plot of C-dots fluorescence vs. the concentration of AFU in human blood serum. Blood serum is less transparent compared to PBS buffer solutions, and thus light absorption or scattering by blood serum is likely to create an inner filter effect which decreases the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. However, the emission position of C-dots (514 nm) used for the assay development was not affected (see supporting information Fig. S5 for C-dots' fluorescence spectra taken in PBS buffer and blood serum). We were able to get comparable results using the assay to detect AFU in human blood serum as compared to PBS buffer. As the concentration of AFU in blood serum increases, the fluorescence of C-dots probe decreased accordingly (Fig. 8A), due to the energy transfer between C-dots and AuNPs brought to close proximity as a result of the increased antigen (AFU) antibody (anti-FUCA2) interaction. Indeed, the Stern-Vølmer plot shows very good linearity between the concentration of AFU in human blood serum and the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of C-dots probe, as the y-intercept was found to be 0.997. Further, the linearity displayed was 0.971, indicating there is indeed a strong correlation with the intensity of fluorescence of the C-dots probe and the concentration of AFU present.

To conclude, an immunoassay based on AuNPs support was developed by monitoring the antigen concentration dependency on a C-dots probe emission. The assay was centered on energy transfer that occurs when the C-dots probe emits photons near the surface plasmon of AuNPs. The concept of the assay was first optimized with a standard antigen, BSA. After optimizing the assay conditions, the AuNPs immunoassay was modified for detection of AFU in PBS buffer. The assay was further used to determine AFU in human blood serum samples, in which a plot of F0/F vs. the concentration of AFU produces a calibration curve that can later be used to determine the concentration of AFU in blood samples. The successful application of this assay in human blood serum is very important since it could potentially be transferred to use for actual patient screening.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the generous support from King Abdulaziz University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and University of Miami, USA.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.08.055.
